role_summary: SNCC field organizer and voter-registration project director in Hale County, Alabama and Natchez, Mississippi.
biographical_paragraph: Annie Pearl Avery's SNCC assignments tracked the geography of Deep South voter registration: directing the Hale County, Alabama project, then staffing the Natchez, Mississippi office [src: 1]. Birmingham was her birthplace in 1943, but her childhood crossed back and forth across the Ohio River, the family moving north to Pittsburgh when she was about nine and returning her to Birmingham at fourteen [src: 1]. By 1961, still a teenager, she was sitting in at a Woolworth's organized by SCLC's Fred Shuttlesworth, and that same year she met James Forman and Julian Bond at a SNCC conference in Georgia [src: 1]. Avery was on the Edmund Pettus Bridge on March 7, 1965, the day Alabama troopers attacked marchers and the day passed into movement memory as Bloody Sunday [src: 1]. On the question of armed self-defense within civil rights organizing, her record is on the record: she has stated that she carried weapons while doing voter-registration work in Mississippi and that the strict-nonviolence orthodoxy was, in her view, tactically wanting [src: 1]. Entry 3 of the Civil Rights History Project at the Library of Congress is her oral history [src: 2].

sources.title: Annie Pearl Avery oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
